hook: Golden torii shrine where the metalworkers' god became the god of money
facts: Founded 1883 on Nishinotoin-dori, north of downtown
Enshrines Kanayamahiko, deity of metals, gold and silver
The torii was repainted gold in 2006
Once frequented by smiths, now by people praying for wealth
w: Smiths once prayed here to the god of metals for help shaping their tools. Then coins arrived, money grew abstract, and a backstreet shrine to blacksmithing quietly became a shrine to cash. The torii went gold in 2006; wallet charms and gold-leaf amulets followed. A neighbourhood altar that followed the money.

hook: Covered arcade at the old end of the Mackerel Road
facts: 164-metre covered arcade, roofed in 1974
Stands where the Saba Kaido or Mackerel Road met Kyoto
Cooperative formed 1977; grocers here since the 1920s
Modelled as the setting for the anime Tamako Market
w: Salted mackerel once travelled here over the mountains from the Sea of Japan, the Saba Kaido ending at this delta where two rivers meet. The fish road faded; the grocers stayed. A tin roof went over the lane in 1974, and tofu sellers, pickle shops and a croquette stand carry on. Kyoto's larder, still trading.

hook: Pine marking where Musashi fought seventy Yoshioka swordsmen
facts: Site of Miyamoto Musashi's 1604 duel with the Yoshioka school
Old pine venerated as a sacred tree beside Hachidai Shrine
Current pine the fourth generation on the spot
The duel ruined the Yoshioka fencing dynasty
legend: They say Musashi killed the boy figurehead first, then cut his way out through the ambush
w: On these eastern outskirts in 1604 the swordsman Musashi met the Yoshioka school by a roadside pine, perhaps seventy archers and blades waiting to end him. They say he struck down their boy figurehead first, then cut his way clear. The old pine, fourth in line, stands enshrined beside Hachidai shrine. Where a fencing dynasty died.

hook: Shopping arcade guarded by handmade monsters
facts: Runs along Ichijo-dori near Kitano Tenmangu
Around thirty yokai sculptures stand outside the shops
Each shop crafts its own monster mascot
Hosts the Ichijo Hyakki Yagyo demon parade, begun 2005
legend: Legend holds that on old Ichijo-dori discarded household objects woke as spirits and paraded by night, the Hyakki Yagyo
w: Legend tells of old Ichijo-dori as the route of the Hyakki Yagyo, the night parade of a hundred demons, where worn-out brooms and pots woke up resentful and marched. The local shopkeepers leaned in: each storefront now fields its own handmade goblin, some thirty of them lining a humble arcade. A grocery run past the monsters.

hook: 300,000 comics shelved inside a 1929 primary school
facts: Housed in the former Tatsuike Primary School, opened 1869
Buildings date from 1928 to 1937, largely untouched
Opened 2006 with Kyoto Seika University
The wall of manga runs to some 200 metres of shelving
w: Tatsuike school went up in 1869, paid for by residents who pinned their hopes on educating their children. The downtown emptied of families and the school closed; rather than knock it down they filled the creaky corridors and tiled stairwells with manga. Read on the lawn or the old wooden floors. A schoolhouse turned reading den.

hook: Two Zen windows and a ceiling of dying men's footprints
facts: Founded 1346 as a Zen retirement hermitage
Round Window of Enlightenment, square Window of Delusion
Ceiling boards taken from blood-soaked Fushimi Castle, 1600
Footprints and bloodstains of seppuku still visible overhead
w: Sit before the round window and the square one: enlightenment and delusion, framed in maple. Then look up. The ceiling boards came from Fushimi Castle's veranda, where in 1600 some 380 cornered loyalists cut themselves open. The blood and footprints rode the timber here, laid overhead to soothe the dead. Beauty with a body count.

hook: Crawl through a paper-smothered stone to sever a bad tie
facts: Enshrines the exiled, embittered Emperor Sutoku
Enkiri-enmusubi stone stands about 1.5 metres tall
Crawl front to back to cut a bond, back to front to forge one
The stone vanishes under thousands of wish slips
legend: They say Sutoku, betrayed and exiled, severed all worldly attachments here, lending the shrine its cutting power
w: An emperor named Sutoku died exiled and furious, and they say his renunciation of all earthly bonds soaked into this Gion shrine. Now people scrawl a wish, crawl front to back through a hole in a boulder to cut a bad relationship, then crawl back to make a good one. The stone disappears under white paper. Heartbreak, on hands and knees.

hook: Temple on the crossroads to hell, with a well to the underworld
facts: Founded 836 at Rokudo-no-Tsuji, the Crossroads of Six Realms
Stands beside Toribeno, an ancient open-air cremation ground
Holds a well said to be courtier Ono no Takamura's gate to hell
A second well of return turned up in 2011 next door
legend: Legend says the courtier Ono no Takamura served the emperor by day and judged the dead alongside King Enma by night, descending through the well
w: The old Kyotoites called this the threshold of the dead, where the cremation fields of Toribeno met the living city. Legend says the courtier Ono no Takamura held a second job: clerk to King Enma of hell, descending each night through a well in the back garden. A second well, the way back, surfaced next door in 2011. Mind the crossroads.

hook: Black-vested kissaten pouring house-roasted coffee since 1932
facts: Opened 1932, originally as Smart Lunch
Run by descendants of the founder, still roasting in-house
Known for hotcakes, French toast and egg sandwiches
Stands on the Teramachi arcade near Sanjo
w: The name meant something in 1932: smart, as in thoughtful service, white shirts and black vests. Three generations on, the roaster still runs in back and the hotcakes arrive the way they always have, on the Teramachi arcade. Dark wood, leather seats, a morning ritual unbothered by the century outside. Coffee that refuses to update.

hook: Bridge-guardian tea house run by the same family since 1160
facts: Founded 1160 beside Uji Bridge by a retired samurai
Reckoned the oldest tea house in Japan
Owners served as guardians of the bridge for centuries
Run today by the 24th generation of the Tsuen family
w: A samurai vassal hung up his sword in 1160 and started pouring tea for travellers at the foot of Uji Bridge, his family doubling as its keepers. Twenty-four generations later they still pour it, having served the shoguns Yoshimasa, Hideyoshi and Ieyasu along the way. Nearly nine centuries, one counter.

hook: Municipal tearoom facing the Phoenix Hall across the river
facts: Run by the city of Uji to promote local tea
Name means teahouse across from the phoenix
Sits on the Uji River bank, minutes from Byodo-in
Serves usucha, koicha and seasonal gyokuro
w: Most tea ceremonies guard their thresholds; this one the town built on purpose, so anyone can kneel and be served properly by a master for the price of a paperback. The name points across the water to the golden phoenix on Byodo-in's roof. Whisked Uji matcha, a seasonal sweet, the river outside. Ceremony without the velvet rope.

hook: Basement kissaten of dark wood, doughnuts and smoke since 1950
facts: Roasting its own beans since 1950
The original room sits down a basement stair; cafe by day, bar by night
Famous for doughnuts made by the owner's wife
A long-time haunt of Kyoto's musicians
w: Find the easily missed stair off the Sanjo Kawaramachi crowd and drop into 1950: wood gone glossy black, low leather banquettes, green ashtrays, the smell of beans roasted on the premises. A coffee house by day, a bar by night, beloved of musicians, with doughnuts from the owner's wife. The basement that time politely ignored.

hook: Cliff temple up 250 steps, lined with grinning raccoon-dogs
facts: Grew around a cave shrine, the main hall rebuilt over a cliff in 1944
Approach guarded by hundreds of ceramic tanuki statues
Built in the kengai overhanging style, like Kiyomizu
Holds a fire-walking festival each summer
legend: They say Musashi trained beneath the temple's waterfall to perfect his two-sword technique
w: Climb 250 steps into the hills above Ichijoji past battalions of pot-bellied ceramic tanuki, each pulling a different face, to a hall bolted over the cliff on stilts. Ascetics trained at its waterfall, and they say Musashi stood under it to master his twin blades. Half pilgrimage, half raccoon-dog cartoon. Faith with a sense of humour.

hook: Last surviving ageya of Kyoto's vanished pleasure quarter
facts: Oldest part dates to its 1641 founding; the rest grew to 1787
The only ageya banquet house left standing in Japan
Held Kyoto's largest banquet hall, kept by the Nakagawa family
Closed in 1985 after 345 years, now a museum
w: In the Shimabara quarter the ageya housed the dearer pleasures: hire a tayu, fill the largest banquet hall in Kyoto, talk poetry and treason. Anti-shogunate plotters and haiku poets drank here. The Nakagawa family ran it from 1641 until 1985, then locked the doors on the last such building in Japan. The pleasure quarter's lone survivor.

hook: Soba house that began as a confectioner in 1465
facts: Founded 1465, reckoned Kyoto's oldest restaurant
Began selling buckwheat sweets, then supplied temple kitchens
Became a soba restaurant only after World War II
Once a purveyor of soba to the Imperial Household
w: In 1465 they sold buckwheat sweets, red bean wrapped in soba dough. Zen temples wanted noodles and lacked the knack for kneading, so the confectioner obliged, and slid sideways into soba over five centuries. The signature horai-soba arrives in stacked lacquer tiers with eight toppings. Imperial purveyors who started out making cakes.

hook: Drill ground of the Shinsengumi swordsmen
facts: Buddhist temple founded 991 in Nakagyo
Served as the Shinsengumi's base and training ground
Holds graves of members and a statue of Kondo Isami
Stages the silent Mibu Kyogen plays, unbroken since 1300
w: The shogunate's enforcers, the Shinsengumi, sharpened their blades on these temple grounds in the 1860s, earning the district's wary nickname, the Mibu wolves. Their graves and a statue of Kondo Isami remain among the cedars. The same temple has staged its masked, wordless Kyogen plays every year since 1300. Where the wolves trained.

hook: Geisha alley one block wide, named for a Portuguese bridge
facts: A narrow lane from Shijo to Sanjo, west of the Kamo
Created by riverbank reclamation in the 1670s
Name from the Portuguese ponte, bridge, written in ateji
Crest a stylised water plover; theatre stages the Kamogawa Odori
w: Reclaimed from the riverbank in the 1670s, this lane stayed barely a body wide and took a Portuguese name, ponte, bridge, spelled in borrowed kanji nobody reads literally. Lanterns, a water-plover crest, geiko slipping between teahouses. The dance theatre has staged the Kamogawa Odori since the 1870s. A river town one block deep.

hook: Market shrine with robot lions that dispense fortunes
facts: Stands at the east end of Nishiki Market
Dedicated to Tenjin, deity of learning
Mechanical shishi lions deliver fortunes for a coin
The automata were designed by the head priest; only ten exist
w: Wedged against the chaos at the end of Nishiki Market sits a small shrine to the god of learning. Feed a coin to the glass case and a mechanical lion-dog jerks to life, dances to court music and drops a paper fortune in the tray. The priest built the contraptions himself; ten of them exist. Folklore, motorised.

hook: Meiji statesman's garden where a war was quietly decided
facts: Villa garden of Prince Yamagata Aritomo, built 1894 to 1898
Designed by master gardener Ogawa Jihei, fed by Lake Biwa canal water
Hosted the 1903 conference before the Russo-Japanese War
Among Kyoto's least-crowded historic gardens
w: Behind a plain wall, a soldier-statesman built a garden of borrowed mountain views and canal-fed streams, the Higashiyama hills pulled right into Ogawa Jihei's design. In 1903 four men sat in the upstairs room and talked Japan into war with Russia. Visitors still walk past, none the wiser. Statecraft over a triple waterfall.

hook: Foothill temple visited by the marathon monks of Mount Hiei
facts: Founded 888 below Mount Hiei, beside Shugaku-in
Enshrines Sekizan Daimyojin, a deity brought from China by Ennin
A waypoint on the brutal thousand-day Kaiho pilgrimage
Only a handful of monks have ever completed the circuit
w: On the bleakest stretch of the thousand-day ordeal, the marathon monks of Hiei, walking themselves to the edge of death over seven years, extend their night circuit down to this quiet temple. Founded 888 around a deity Ennin carried from China, it blazes red in late autumn. A stop on the path to enlightenment, or collapse.

hook: Forest mausoleum of the emperors, the Mi-tera
facts: Rebuilt 1226 and named for a spring that gushed on the grounds
Resting place of Emperor Shijo and dozens of imperial dead
Butsuden rebuilt 1668 in Chinese Song-dynasty style
Holds Japan's largest nirvana painting, 8 by 16 metres
w: They call it the Mi-tera, the imperial temple, and the title is literal: more than two dozen emperors lie in the forest behind it, Shijo first in 1242. A spring still wells up where the temple took its name. The hall, raised in 1668, copies Song-dynasty China; inside hangs an eight-by-sixteen-metre dying Buddha. Crowd-free, by death.

hook: Baroque cafe built like an ocean liner's grand saloon
facts: Founded 1934 near Shijo-Kiyamachi
First cafe in Japan made a Registered Tangible Cultural Property, 2003
Interior modelled on a luxury liner's hall, domed and stained-glass
A wartime refuge for talk of peace, art and literature
w: An Italian student and some young painters dreamed up the room in the 1930s as the saloon of a luxury liner, all domed ceiling and pointed stained glass. Under the militarist years it doubled as a safe corner to speak quietly of peace and books. The pudding and velvet outlasted the century. Coffee aboard a ship that never sails.
